page: Vultr voc-s-24c-192gb-5760s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute server configured with an AMD EPYC-Rome processor. This x86_64 architecture system features 24 vCPUs and 24 physical cores operating at a base clock speed of 2.0 GHz with a shared CPU allocation. The hardware includes 192.0 GB of system memory, providing a ratio of 8.0 GB of RAM per core. Local storage is bundled with 5760 GB of NVMe SSD capacity, and the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address.

In synthetic performance evaluations, the server demonstrates strong processing capabilities. The stress-ng single-core CPU benchmark recorded a score of 2922.47, placing it in the top 25% of tested instances. Multi-core performance under stress-ng reached 69366.22, which also ranks in the top 25%. The CPU supports advanced instruction sets including AVX, AES, SHA_NI, BMI2, and FMA, though it lacks AVX-512 extensions.

The server offers high resource density, combining a large memory footprint and substantial NVMe storage capacity on a shared CPU model. This configuration presents a qualitative cost-efficiency advantage for workloads requiring massive storage and memory without needing dedicated CPU cores. It is highly suited for large-scale databases, memory-intensive data processing, caching layers, and storage-heavy enterprise applications.
description: Vultr voc-s-24c-192gb-5760s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute server powered by an AMD EPYC-Rome processor. Operating on the x86_64 architecture, it features 24 vCPUs and 24 physical cores at 2.0 GHz with a shared CPU allocation. The system is equipped with 192.0 GB of RAM, yielding 8.0 GB per core, and includes a bundled 5760 GB NVMe SSD for high-capacity local storage. In benchmark testing, the server achieved strong results, with stress-ng single-core and multi-core scores of 2922.47 and 69366.22 respectively, both placing in the top 25%. Supported instruction sets include AVX, AES, and SHA_NI. This combination of high memory capacity and extensive NVMe storage makes the instance highly efficient for memory-intensive workloads, large databases, and data processing tasks that benefit from substantial local storage.
